prompt: When are two formulas logically equivalent?

options.text: When their final truth-table columns match in every row.
When they use the same variables in the same order.
When they have the same main operator.

explanation: Logical equivalence depends on identical final columns, not on appearance or shared symbols.

statement: If two final columns differ in one row, the formulas are not logically equivalent.
explanation: Equivalence requires a perfect row-by-row match. One mismatch is enough to break equivalence.

prompt: Two formulas are logically equivalent only if their final columns are ___ in every row.
explanation: The test for equivalence is full agreement in the final columns.

prompt: Classify each pair using the given final columns.

items.text: `T F T F` and `T F T F`
`T T F F` and `T F F F`
`F F T T` and `F F T T`
`T T T F` and `T T F F`

explanation: Pairs are equivalent only when every row in the two final columns matches exactly.

prompt: What should you compare to decide whether $\sim (P \vee Q)$ and $(\sim P \wedge \sim Q)$ are logically equivalent?

options.text: The number of symbols in each formula.

explanation: Logical equivalence is determined by comparing the final columns, because those columns give the truth values of the whole formulas.
